text: Manoj Bajpuri used to live in Ramada because he was the star.
Nawaz, Richa, Dineet.
We all used to get one car and we used to get in the car.
There was no gym and all.
So like, you know, we would get in the car and there was a stadium.
I used to go to the stadium and do running and Nawaz would make us do PT.
And we would just try and like burn some calories.
Thank you so much for doing this with us, Uma.
And welcome to the Humans of Bombay show.
It's called How the Hell Did I Do It?
Thank you so much for having me.
We're all very curious about how the hell did you do it.
But I'm going to start with a very iconic statement that you made in one of your interviews,
which has intrigued all of us.
You said something like, I'm tired of being rescued by heroes.
I want to be the hero.
And I think that that's so amazing.
So can you tell us a little bit about that?
I think I was misquoted.
I think I said, I am the hero.
I am the hero.
Not I want to be, I am.
Yeah, even better.
Even better. Even better.
So where did that stem from?
I don't know.
It just, I think sometimes like when I find somebody like you,
I kind of vibe with and get along.
I just talk more candidly.
And I feel like, yes, I did reach a point in my career where I was like,
yeah, what am I doing?
Like, I mean, I want to do more.
And as an actor, I have more to offer.
So it was coming from that sense because normally in like films,
ek hero hota hai and then, you know, he's the story is about him and how he's
and then the female actor is always like an ancillary to his journey.
And his dream and his conflict.
And I feel like, no, I mean, like, you know, I would I want to also do
sure, this is a great script and I really believe in I want to be a part of course,
I'll still do that.
But I feel like I also want to do films which are about real living,
breathing, three dimensional women with their own journeys and conflict
and dreams and aspirations.
And in that sense, I feel like,
you know, that's where the straight that statement came from.
That's yeah.
And I mean, there has been a evolution also.
There are more female women centric roles that are now happening in Bollywood.
So that's amazing.
But also, was it kind of like a foreshadowing?
Because we we know growing up, you came from a very conservative family.
So is that something that you felt since the beginning?
Or what was your childhood like?
Wow. OK. Fun, actually, a lot of fun.
My mom, my mom is from Kashmir and my dad is from Delhi.
They had this love marriage.
So I as a child, I had like the best of all the words.
Like we would go to like a regular South Delhi school.
Weekends used to be at my dadi's house, which is in Zambudin.
You know, so I had to like really behave like a good
demure girl.
And then and then summer vacations when my nana nani were alive,
used to go to Kashmir and, you know, spend time with them in Gurrey's and Bara Mullah.
And you know, that was a completely different world.
So I think while growing up, me and my brother,
we just have a constantly like part of different worlds.
And we had to really adapt to sort of.
And we were always like very keen to be loved and liked by everybody.
So I think that's where the acting thing came in,
to be able to assimilate no matter where you are.
And yeah, just a regular childhood,
like listen to Backstreet Boys, watch the 90s films.
Shah Rukh Khan was my favorite actor, you know, that kind of stuff.
When I was in college first year, I think even although in school also
I did dramatics, but college, I think really changed for me
because I really like, you know, freedom and travelling alone
and going to like North Campus, because I was in Gargi College, South Campus,
going over there for different competitions and meeting kids from all over.
I think I just I just exploded in the sense that I was like,
oh, wow, like there's so much out there.
It's a whole new world.
It's a whole new world from very like sheltered family.
You know, I just completely was like, oh, wow, there's so much I can do.
And I feel like that explosion is continuing.
So when you made this switch, your father's a rest.
Like he owns a chain of restaurants, mother's a homemaker.
You are this brilliant.
I mean, you're academically inclined.
And then you go to study history, which is like arts, humanity.
And then you're like in theater.
Right. So it's like a fluctuation.
Right. Almost.
What was the reaction of your parents?
You know, what was the what was the immediate
like kind of response to this this new found love?
They were fairly unhappy about it.
They were like, you know, why didn't you like an actual career?
But we can also like like an actual career.
Like theater is not a real career.
And at that point in time, you know, I was also extremely shy about
even admitting it to myself that I wanted to act, you know, coming from like
a Delhi household with no connections to the film industry
to tell them, oh, I want to be an actress.
Yeah. The classic reaction would be like, you want to be a heroine?
Yeah. I mean, you want to go to Mumbai.
Like how like weird and random is that?
Like, who do you even know in the city of Mumbai or Bollywood or whatever?
And I was like, I don't, but I really enjoy doing this.
I in fact, I started getting a lot of calls for auditions,
except when I was doing theater.
So I was like, you know, people are calling me and I'm just a kid,
like, you know, doing theater, like in Delhi.
So maybe you've got something.
So they tried to dissuade me.
And then I told my dad, now they listen, I really want to try this.
Because if you don't let me try this, then, you know, I obviously won't do it.
But I always like resent it and always like hold it in my heart.
And maybe when I'm older, I do like grandmother parts.
So like, at some point I'll express this, but I always feel like
my family didn't support me in this decision.
And my father's a softy, like in the sense like,
you know, I think everyone says at least that I'm the only one who can
manipulate him emotionally, which I do quite often.
So I told him, OK, then I won't do it.
And all I've made the sad face, you know, what?
Just go try it out.
Do it for a year. If it works out great, if it doesn't.
Don't keep struggling forever, because I think most
parents have this fear that their kids are just going to keep like
yeah, hustling and struggling and nothing's going to come out of it.
So they don't keep doing that.
I don't want my daughter to put herself through that.
So try it for a year. If it happens great.
If it doesn't happen, just come back and do something concrete and solid with your life.
Yeah. So that's how it started.
And I was just fortunate I came to Mumbai.
Of course, I had the usual struggling period, but it wasn't so bad, to be honest,
because I started doing a lot of like commercials, ads.
And when IPL used to come, whatever, so suddenly I'll pop up
and you'll be selling like a cream or a phone or something.
So they're like, oh, she's doing something.
Yeah, she's there. She's coming on TV.
Yeah, she's coming. It means something.
No, but when you took this decision to come to Mumbai as a 22 year old girl
without any connections in Bollywood
or, you know, the industry,
how did you manage?
So you came here, you didn't have a plan because, you know, there were.
No, I had a plan. My plan was to do one film.
I was like, I want to just do one film so I can prove it to everyone.
I can do this.
Like I did it. Like I did it.
See, I told you I could do it.
There was a plan really after that.
But yeah, it was just that excitement of like,
you know, coming to a new city.
I'd never lived alone.
I had never done a lot of things for myself,
because when you're like growing up with family,
though, everything happens on its own.
Like you come back from school, you throw your clothes in a corner.
And magically, it's folded, washed and kept in the cupboard.
And I was like, wow.
So first time when I moved here, just to also like run a household,
what it means to run a household and, you know, pay your taxes and,
you know, if your car's not working, like you have to go and fix it.
So all those things.
And I was like, oh, my God, this is a lot of hard work being an adult.
You know, and we take our parents so much for granted.
And I think I should always get cranky at my mother.
Like, why are you always discussing, like, you know, domestic help issues?
And like, you know, now I really understand, you know, what all
grownups need to do, because I'm now doing it for myself.
And it's a lot because you have to run your career and you at the same time,
you know, also run your life, which is actually, frankly speaking, quite a lot.
Yeah. And how are you sustaining financially?
So initial months, I took money from my father.
In fact, for a good deal, I took 30,000 rupees from my father,
which was a fairly decent amount because I calculated,
I'm not going to splurge. I'm not going to overdo this.
This is my rent. This is much food.
Blah, blah, blah. And like my dad, are you sure?
Do you want more money? I said, no, no, no, no, no.
I think this is good enough.
And I don't want to like, you know, push it.
I don't know. Also feel like a leech.
I am also aware that so many people come to this city to struggle
and they don't come from such a, you know, financially stable background.
I wouldn't say we had a lot of money while growing up, but we were always like,
comfortable, comfortable.
And also, like I knew that my parents were doing this and
they were not entirely happy with it, but they were still like,
but they were still supportive.
So I sort of felt like I had to earn this.
But yeah, but it's a good feeling, actually, to be economically independent
and to make your own money.
It's a big high.
And I feel like the ability that you now have money
and you can buy things for your loved ones or do things for them
or take them out on vacations.
I think that for me is very important.
In that sense, I really enjoy the power that money gives you,
the ability to do things, not just for yourself,
but for other people who you care about and you love.
Yeah. Yeah.
And do you remember your first audition?
I know one of the first initial large brands you signed was Unilever.
And of course, it's a household name.
Therefore, by default, you would become a household face
because you're selling their products.
Do you remember the audition? Do you remember what it was like?
Do you remember your first paycheck?
Walk me through a little bit about that.
Very clearly.
Early days, I had gone to this production, it was called Chrome Pictures.
Aamir Sharma was now a big film director as well.
He's done Tevar and Badhaidu and all these films.
You know, it's his company.
And I had gone there to audition for something else.
And I remember sort of doing the audition and coming back home
and then I got a call the next day saying,
okay, that didn't work for you, but there's another ad.
You want to come and like do another audition?
So I said, okay, I'm not doing anything.
Things have gone all the way and Bandra was their office, I remember back then.
So I had gone there and had another audition and
two days later, I get this call saying, it's on.
And it was a very big ad with a mobile company that Abhishek Bachchan used to endorse.
I was very excited.
I'm like, yes, but also at that point in time, you know,
there's so many times you get shortlisted
and then you would never make the final cut.
So I was really disappointed.
And I kept telling my mom about it and then it would not happen.
And then, you know, I would feel disheartened,
but she would feel even more disheartened.
So I was damn excited because it was an ad with Abhishek.
Bachchan and like it was this thing.
So I went and auditioned and then they called me and said, you're on.
And then they told me they had a budget of five thousand bucks.
I was like, it's okay.
I don't care about the money.
I just want to do the ad.
I want to do the ad. I want to get my first gig.
And I remember we shot at Whistling Woods and that was my first commercial.
With Abhishek Bachchan?
With AB and he was very sweet.
I remember there was a mark on the floor.
So they've called me, of course, to my van.
So I've come like very excited to give my first shot.
And I see the mark and I go stand there.
And then AB comes in and says, darling, that's my spot.
Sorry, sir. Sorry. Sorry.
And we laugh about it now.
Because I remind him of that story.
He's like, you're that actor who takes everyone's spot.
No, but he was a thorough gentleman.
I really had a wonderful time.
And of course, now Amit Sharma's.
Then I worked again with his company a few days, a few months later on another commercial.
So I think that was the starting.
And then after that, I was doing a lot of like commercial and ad work.
And so what was your mother's reaction when she finally saw you with an Abhishek Bachchan in the ad?
I called her when I left from there.
I was in Nautarikshan. I called her saying, mama, I did my first commercial.
And she was like, really? Congratulations.
She was very happy about it.
You also auditioned for Tariz Amin, but I think for Amir Khan's girlfriend back in the day.
What was that process like?
Walk us through a little bit about what it's like for a girl with no connections in the industry to go on these auditions, right?
Yeah, but you know what happened?
I'll tell you, it was a very funny story because I was doing a lot of theatre work in Delhi.
So I was doing college theatre.
We were directing on plays.
I would go for my Act 1 rehearsal in the afternoon.
Also, there's a group called Jamghat, which is a group I'm still associated with.
It's a group of, it's an NGO that works with street kids.
So we used to do a lot of workshops with them and I used to take our time.
We used to also try and get them to evolve their own scripts and do their own play.
And then we used to do a fundraiser every year where the kids would direct.
I would mentor them, but they would direct and write their own play and set it up.
And then we sell tickets and that's how it was like a self-sustaining model.
So we did one of those shows and then in the end they call you and be like,
This is the director, she did the workshop for the kids.
And I'm still in college, I've managed to wear my fab India kurta,
it's an orange curly hair at that point in time.
That was my look in college.
And somebody saw that play, Tanmay, and he was assisting on Tariz MB.
And the next two, three days later I was in Mumbai.
I was here on a show with Amir Azhars and another play that I was doing.
So I get a call and I'm like,
Hi, I'm calling from Amir Khan Productions and we saw your play.
And we're casting for this part and it's very similar.
It's not a very big part, but it's something similar to what you do.
So I'm thinking somebody is doing some prank call.
Some friend is doing some prank call.
So I'm like, Oh, Amir Khan Productions.
Let's do this, I'll come and meet Mr Khan.
Thinking in my head, this is just a friend doing Firkhi.
And I put the phone down, gone back to sleep.
Then I'm a few days later, next day I'm at the box,
NCPA and a friend of mine was doing a play there.
So just going to check it out to see what it's like.
And Sunday I get a call saying, can you come to our office now?
It's in Bandra or Khar whatever.
And I'm just like, where is Bandra?
Like I'm actually not from Bombay.
And Sunday I'm like, Oh my God, this is real.
And of course, I said, I'm at NCPA, so don't worry, it's too far.
We reschedule.
So now I'm nervous because I'm like, Oh God, this is for real.
Like, what am I going to do?
And I had no idea.
So when they called me and we set up the time,
I was my best maroon fab India kutta.
And I went for that test and I think it went horribly
because I didn't land the part.
But I was just so excited.
And I still remember, so Chitra Pillai was there
and that office at point time.
So I was very starstruck.
I'm like, that's Chitra Pillai, oh my God,
like how cool is that?
Because like a Delhi kid, right?
I was starved of any kind of movie star connection.
I think that's even any kid, like movie star.
It's like there's Chitra Pillai and then somewhere inside
there was also Imran Khan.
So I was like, oh wow, like that's Imran Khan.
Like, you know, I was just super starstruck.
Of course, it didn't work out and whatever,
but then Tanmay became a very dear friend
and you know, he's still a very dear friend.
So that's just how it happened.
And then, you know, I was like, you know, maybe I can,
if I try a little bit and like focus.
It's the opportunities are opening up.
Yeah, like it's there, right?
Like if they're calling me,
then I must be doing something right.
And then the big break, Anurag Kashyap spotted you
and you landed Gangs of Vasupur, which is just iconic.
I think everybody's just a fan of that movie.
I'm not a single person being like, you know,
like we don't like that movie.
You're just not human if that happens.
So walk us through that.
How was that?
What was that?
And what was the level of starstruck there?
Cause I feel like, okay, maybe it was different back then,
but right now they're all just legends, right?
You see the labyrinth?
No, it was, he was a legend.
And then I had seen Gulal and I had seen Black Friday
and I was like, I remember seeing Black Friday in Delhi
and I was like, oh my God, who was his director?
And I really, you know, like I would really like
to work with him.
That just thought pops in your head, right?
And then I come to Bombay and then, you know,
you don't even know how to access people
because you're just like literally a newcomer.
You don't know people.
I didn't have a management agency.
I didn't have, I had no people around me.
I would just get these random messages from coordinators
saying, here in Aramnagar, so and so,
there's an audition, you can go there.
And I remember I used to have this bag
where I had a white shirt, a black dress,
like a Neel and black dress,
and one Indian suit type outfit.
Because I knew like by now, like I figured out
that okay, one of these will work for the look
because the look was very important.
And they tell you, okay, you have to wear Western,
or you have to wear Indian,
or you have to wear like a dress.
Formal.
Formal, so I was like, I can't think of this every day.
And like your means were also limited.
So I bought these three things.
I was like, this is standard.
This will go with everything.
And I used to carry that bag and used to go
from one studio, which is like right down the road actually,
and go for these auditions.
And like they had these tiny small changing rooms
and change there, do my own makeup,
and do my own hair, which, you know,
sometimes with disastrous results,
but I mean, this is how you learn.
And yeah, and I remember Tia used to be this girl,
used to cast at that point in time for a lot of commercials.
And she called me and she does an ad with,
again, it was an ad for a mobile phone with Amir Khan.
What is it with your mobile phone?
I don't know, I don't know.
You should just sign me or whatever.
Every mobile phone I'm there.
They just do more commercials than the others, I don't know.
And then again, this was a commercial.
I was playing Amir Khan's girlfriend,
finally after all these years.
That Alarak Kashyap directed.
And yeah, so I did that audition, I came back,
got a call a few days later saying,
Alarak sir wants to meet you,
gone for the meeting.
He just said one thing, don't put too much makeup.
Just be as is.
Just be like as is, so I was like, okay.
And by that time I've also done like some good 10, 20 ads.
Like I was doing a lot of commercial work, right?
And I think that was Kashyap's first commercial.
As a commercial director.
And I remember I got ready and all that,
and he saw me, he's like, wash your face.
So I'm like, wash my face.
And he's actually, we were shooting in white,
he literally made me wash my face in front of him.
Oh wow.
So I was like, sir, this is your first commercial,
you're wearing so much makeup, you don't know.
So he was like, shut up, do what I'm saying, quietly.
So yeah, that was that, and it was,
the ad was over three, four days.
And then I think by the second day he told me,
I'm gonna cast you in a film.
So then I'm, me being dumb, I'm stupid.
I was like, it doesn't work like that.
I've heard that you have to struggle a lot.
I've just come, it's only been about a year,
and you know, I have heard that you have to struggle a lot.
Then he's like, are you crazy?
I said, yes.
He's like, I'm giving up.
I couldn't believe it.
I couldn't believe it was so easy.
I was like, no, no, you're saying this.
It doesn't work like that.
I've heard that you have to struggle a lot.
I've just been, I've come here, I've been doing commercials.
It can't be so easy.
I've heard of films with Anurag Kashyap.
Let me struggle a little bit.
Are you sure?
He's like, you're mad.
Yeah, but I do want it.
I'm giving it to you.
Whenever I've really worked super hard for something,
it just, it just goes.
But when I'm just doing something, it just lands.
So I think that works for me somehow.
So anyway, so he said that I'm gonna cast you in a film.
Yeah.
And then?
And then he did.
And then Gangs of Vasirpur was happening.
And how was it like your first day on a film set?
Wait, wait, first day was a long way off.
The workshop process has started.
And two actors, me and Piyush Mishra were told,
don't give him the script.
So we were like, and then there was this workshop
and we were languishing, okay, not knowing any better.
And then we realized everybody else has got the script.
So then I asked the first-year student to teach me the script.
What's happening?
I had no idea.
So he said, sir, I won't tell you the problem.
Like, please teach me because everybody knows
what they're doing and I don't.
And then I read the script and I was like,
I don't have my roadie in this.
You know, it says, Fezal Mohsina, go to the market.
Which market?
What market?
What they do?
Nothing.
So I'm a bit like, okay, like...
What am I doing?
What am I doing?
So then I kind of...
Then Kashyap got to know that, you know, I'd read the script.
So you read it, right?
I said, sir, I had to know because everyone knows
what they're doing, they're in relation to what.
I have no idea.
People are asking me in the workshop, are there actors?
And I'm just looking dumb.
So it was just also that, right?
So he said, no, no, no, I'm like, you went through,
because Piyush Pisha, again, like, you know,
he's like the sutradhar of both the parts.
And he was evolving that part.
And like, this was also a part where he wanted me
and Nawaz to improv a lot.
So hence it was written in that sense.
It would depend on the actor chemistry as opposed
to like writing scenes and like, you know,
making it like, rigid.
With the flow, raw, authentic.
Correct.
So then I remember having this very like,
what should I call it?
Sentimental conversations, saying, sir,
I really want to be an actor.
And like, my family's not for this.
And I really want to make them proud.
And I really am a huge fan of your work.
But like, if there's not much for me to do in this,
I'm sure like, we'll work on something together.
So I really want my first film to be something special.
Like I really, that kind of thing.
So he's looking at me like, are you crazy?
There is for Anubhuti's sister, who's also now a director.
And she was assisting at that point in time.
And all the other ADC's were like,
I was like, no, I'm not saying no.
I'm just saying that I don't think I have much of a role.
I don't know what I'm doing in this film.
Yeah, I don't know what I'm doing in this film.
So he's like, just trust me and just do this film.
You will not regret it.
Yeah.
So and then, I don't know,
something just felt right about it.
And I was like, okay, fine.
Let's just go with it.
And although like, I was still lost
because I had no idea what we were doing.
It's like this two part film
with like 300 actors.
And like, it is not how traditionally films are made.
Of course.
But it was, the process was so fun.
And I think I worked with such an incredible lot
of really super talented and special people
who all now are doing so, so well
in their individual journeys and paths, you know.
Almost all the ADs in that film have turned directors.
Vasan, in fact, I judged Monica,
which was an AD on that film.
What a film.
Vicky Kaushal has done Clap for me with that film.
You know, so all of us, it's like so like beautiful.
All the actors in Jaideet Pallava to like,
Nawaz to Richa, everybody's doing so, so well.
So I feel it was just that very special film
where so many people just came together
and who were just starting their careers out.
And now they all have gone into like different,
obviously, they were all newcomers.
We had no idea.
And I remember we were staying in a really rundown
guest house called Swati International
or Pooja International, I'm forgetting.
I've written it down somewhere,
but I think it was Swati International.
It was a really rundown guest house,
like really as you can imagine.
And then I remember Manoj Bajper used to live in Ramada
because he was the star.
So he was there and all of us were in Swati International.
And then you couldn't complain because Kashyap was,
and Nawaz were living opposite us
in an even worse guest house.
So we were like the directors saying that,
then you can't even complain about this.
You know, so we were, and then Nawaz, Richa,
Vineet, Anurima, and one more person, I'm forgetting.
We all used to get one car and we used to get in the car.
There was no gym and all.
So like, you know, we would get in the car
and there was a stadium.
I used to go to the stadium and do running
and Nawaz would make us do PT
and we would just try and like burn some calories.
It was, I'm telling you, it was like,
I don't know what you call it, like calisthenics or whatever,
like, you know, hand exercises and like,
just try and get some cardio in and get your heart rate up.
But like, and I just thought that's how movies got made.
Like, I didn't even know.
When I, I think, when they asked me to do a photo shoot
and they showed me some pictures
and this is whatever with you and Nawaz,
that's when I realised, oh, I'm the leader of this film or what?
I swear, it was like, I was like, oh,
and then I remember seeing, even when part one came out,
I was not on any of the holdings or posters or whatever.
And I thought I would be in one scene in part one,
like, you know, literally that permission scene.
And I remember taking my parents to watch the film in Delhi
and I'm not there in the first half,
so I'm telling dad, like, you know,
I have one scene, but in the second one, I'm there more.
So don't feel bad and all about it.
Because now you know how to explain a gangster
versus who experienced your parents,
because even I didn't understand it.
There was no precedent for it
or there hasn't been a film like that after.
So my parents were like, oh, I'm rubbish when she's done.
She's not even there in this film.
And like, you know, she's saying, part two will come,
now part two, you made it.
So they couldn't fag them at all,
like, how did you make it, it hasn't been made yet,
it hasn't been hit yet, how did you make it?
So I'm like, I also don't know, but we made it.
We'll figure.
We'll figure.
No, but we had shot both the parts together, right?
But they were like, but this is not come,

Patience was not something that you chose, it was just thrust upon you.
You had to wait for everything to happen in your life.
I still remember at Delhi University, I used to stand in line for three to four hours
every month to get a bus pass costing 13 rupees that used to give us free unlimited rides on DTC,
which was the only way we could travel.
Travel, yeah.
Because there was no money for an auto.
And because of that, I think we learned what patience means.
And we learned that good things take time.
And we used to crib at that point of time, but I think that was such a wonderful
blessing in disguise.
Where today's kids for no fault of theirs do not know that.

You need a book, click.
You need clothes, click.
You need food, clicks.
You need the latest movies, click.
You need love, swipe right.
Everything that you need like right there when you want it.
